paragraphs.text: In that same year Sarai, seeing her barrenness, said to Abram: “Behold, the Lord hath restrained me from bearing: go in, I pray thee, unto my maid (Gen 16:2); by her perhaps I shall obtain consolation.” And since Sarai was not at rest, although Abram was slow to fulfill her counsel, yet, as it is written, “Abram hearkened to the voice” of his wife (Gen 16:2), and Sarai gave him Hagar the Egyptian, given with the other handmaids by Pharaoh on the day when he took her to wife.

When Hagar conceived, she began to despise her mistress, thinking that her seed would enter the land of promise and receive it as inheritance. Sarai might have bridled Hagar’s pride, but since she saw that the one who had been a maid up until then, since the day she was given to Abram, had shared his bed, she did not rebuke the concubine, that she might not bring dishonor upon Abram himself. Yet she said to him: “My wrong is upon thee” (Gen 16:5) — that is, I did not exchange thee for a king, and yet thou hast now preferred a maid to me; Hagar ought to thank me, for my prayer was heard and she conceived, because I gave her to thee that I might receive consolation through her; but she for the good done to her by me returns evil, in the eyes of all the handmaids inflicts on me a grievous insult.

Hagar, who had hoped in Abram, when she saw that he had given her into the hands of the mistress whom she had reproached, was afraid and fled. But an angel met her and said: “Return to thy mistress, and submit thyself under her hand… thy seed shall be multiplied, and shall not be numbered for multitude… for the Lord hath heard thy affliction” (Gen 16:9–11) — if thou art ready to return and serve thy mistress, “and thou shalt bear a son, and shalt call his name Ishmael” (Gen 16:11). “He will be a wild ass of a man,” because he will dwell in the wilderness, and like a wild ass will not be at peace with men; “his hand against every man, that is, to plunder all, and every man’s hand against him” (Gen 16:12). Since he will attack all the nations, he himself will be attacked by all the nations. “And on the borders of all his brethren, that is, of the sons of Sarah and Keturah, shall he dwell” (Gen 16:12), because to the sons of Shem the lands of their inheritance had already been allotted.

Hagar understood that Abram would have many children, but not by her. She was assured of this by the fact that he no more came in unto her and, as soon as he noticed that she had conceived, did not draw near her. Abram only at Sarai’s wish had come in to Hagar, that he might have through Hagar even a little consolation for her, until God should gladden her with the fruit of her own womb. And Hagar said: “Thou art the God of vision, who condescendest and showest Thyself to those who honor Thee.” And further she said: “I have seen a vision after Thou hast seen me” (Gen 16:13) — for at first the angel, when he appeared, said nothing to her, lest he frighten her. But when he addressed her, then she had a vision within the vision, that is, in the person of the angel God appeared; therefore she called the well: “The well of the Living One who seeth me” (Gen 16:14). Hagar returned to her mistress and asked her forgiveness. And when she told Abram and Sarai of her vision, telling them what the angel had said to her about her son — namely that he would live on the borders of his brethren — then this news, heard by Sarai, blotted out the grief which Hagar had caused her earlier. “And Hagar bore… and Abram called his son’s name… Ishmael” (Gen 16:15), as he was taught by Hagar.
